speaker: interviewer
text: '96. The survivor is Meyer Cyker. Meyer, M-E-Y-E-R. Cyker, C-Y-K-E-R. And this is tape number 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: This life went on, working in the labor camp, basically, and it went from until about June 1942. People started hearing that the [INAUDIBLE] group over there, the Ukrainian and Lithuanian militiamen were liquidating cities in the area over Wolyn, which is like a state, a state, and the small towns. People were getting very nervous not knowing what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, the Germans moved all the Jews to one place and call it ghetto. I believe it could have been from March or April or June. We heard that in Rivne, they took thousands of Jews down to the Pine Valley, which was a valley with pine trees, and they shot them in cold blood. I do have a picture I got from my trip recently where they show the footsteps, you know? This was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. In between '42 in June and October, was a very nervous situation. People were talking how to escape, where to run away, to go out to the forests. Some young men went to the forests, but if they looked for them, they would kill their families. It was not a simple situation. At that time, my father received a visitor from a village close to [NON-ENGLISH], which is a small village near Sdoipaca, S-D-O-I-P-A-C-A, Sdoipaca. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he had a farm, this Polish farmer, and he came and told my dad that he heard in October, they're going to liquidate the Zdolbunow ghetto. And he wants him to escape and run away. It was an interesting story behind it. In 1921, when my dad served in the army in Krakow, this Polish farmer, who was married to a Ukrainian girl, was drafted. And apparently, my father was very nice to him because he didn't let the Polish officer abuse him. He never forgot it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took out my dad and my mother from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the ghetto, and he took him into the forest, and they built a bunker for them, you know, not far from his farm. A couple of days later, my brother and I jumped off a truck near the forest. We both escaped to the forest. And we spent our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] youth, you know, until February 1944 in the forest, hiding in the farms, first in this guy's farm, then when things got difficult because of the movements of the Germans and the Ukrainian militiamen, Polish militiamen, everybody was fighting everybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Russian partisans and a lot of Jewish groups who escaped from the ghettos, organized parties and groups of 20 to 50 people, doing sabotage work, blowing up the German trains, going to the Russian front between 1942 and '44. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And as it happened, on October the 13th, we heard the machine guns, and we saw the burning of the ghetto. The militia and SS came into the ghetto, start killing and shooting people. The Jewish men burned the ghetto trying to escape because of the smoke not far from the forest. Quite a few people were able to escape, unfortunately, some of them were killed in the forest by the Ukrainians that worked for the Germans. And they were not able to survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fortunately, my family survived, thanks to the farmers and the relationship my father and my family [INAUDIBLE] had with the farmers. The farmers had great respect for our family, for my parents and my grandparents. My grandfather vanished in the ghetto. I know that-- I heard that-- we were told that he, he fought against being killed. He tried to shock some Germans and Ukrainians who were very strong men. My grandfather served in the Tsarist Army in the front line. He was over 6 feet tall, a handsome, very tough individual. And he vanished. My two uncles on my father's side were able to join the Russian Army, and they escaped. They escaped a few months before the ghetto. Their wives-- and each of them had three children-- were killed. My grandmother in Kostopol, my uncles, my cousins, my aunts were all killed. They, they also liquidated Kostopol. And as a result, that area became Judenrein. The last one to be liquidated, the last standing ghetto in this area was Zboriv. This is because they needed the Jew that worked for the [NON-ENGLISH] and it seems that Graber, Ernst Graber, the engineer, was making them false, you know, documents as Aryans. And he tried to save as many Jews as he could. The story of Graber is well documented in the book written by a minister. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] by February or March of 1944, the Russian army came in and liberated that part of Poland, Rivne and Zboriv in the area. Unfortunately, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German army was surrounded by the Russians and still put up a fight. And we came out of the forest. We were greeted very nicely, very warmly by the Russian soldiers and officers. We went back to our home. The [INAUDIBLE] was clean, prepared by the neighbors, you know, in the [INAUDIBLE] condition. And all the people that came out from the woods, we found about 30 to-- 30 people that escaped in different parts of the forest, which we did not know about them, came into our house. One of the Russian officers said that for you to be safe, you should move more east. And we were able to move more east. He prepared for us a train, you know, not a passenger train, but a train. We moved further away from the front where the Russian army was still fighting with the Germans and attacking, liquidating the German armies. We spend in the village, a small town like [NON-ENGLISH], several months. I went in to the-- we went in, my brother and I, to the school, tried to catch up on a little bit of education. And from there, due to the new order that everybody who was born on the Polish side had a right to go back to Poland, go west. And the family decided to go west, so we came west first in Poland. And from Poland, we went to Vienna, from Vienna, we went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you continue, how much time did you spend in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: From October '42 till '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell us some of the things that happened and how you lived in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the forest was a very interesting life. We, we slept with the cattle and horses and the cows at night so we could keep warm. And the farmers, to be safe, docked some bunkers underneath their things, so in the daytime, we used to hide over there. Unfortunately, when the-- somebody came in close to the forest, when the war was continuing, at times we had to leave one farm and run to another farm away from the fighting groups. It was a lot of partisans in the forest, but we were quite young, and they didn't accept young fellows, you know, to, to do any military work.

speaker: interviewer
text: The partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because by that time, the-- all the Jewish groups have joined more experience. Russian partisans, professional that the Russians dropped off, you know, into the-- sending into the forest. Also many Russian escapees from the soldiers, escapees and officers from the prison camps have joined the forest. Many Ukrainians, which were pro-Russian have joined the forest, some Poles. It was a hectic life in the forest for-- I mean, hectic in the way there was always fighting going go on. Basically, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was a man operate there by the name of Kuznitzov, Nikolai Kuznitzov. He was a Russian dressed like a German, a blond that spoke perfect German. And he used to go out and kill, sabotage, and kill German officers. And another fellow was a fellow Nachum Kohn, a Jewish man that escaped from Warsaw. He was a refugee [NON-ENGLISH]. He joined up with Kuznitzov and he heroically fought with these groups against the Germans, know that they could advance on the, on the Ukrainians that cooperated with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 12-- you were 14 to 16 years old at this time between--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. 12 to 16.

speaker: interviewee
text: 13 and a half to 16, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your brother all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had escaped with another girl from our town. And her name was Baka Vietzkah. In one of the raids in the farm, we had to separate. We all ran away in different directions. And she wound up in a Czech farm. And we found out-- I saw her after the war. She finally landed in Israel. And we found several people from the forest that were saved by Czechs. Some of them married a Czech, young men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have different farms that you had relationships with the people, so you would move from one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't have any relationship [INAUDIBLE]. We escaped from our farm. We tried to find-- we knew the names of decent farmers like this-- the last farmer, Karfczuk, he was a Seventh Day Adventist. He was quite educated for a farmer. And he knew my grandfather. And he knew our father. And we came in over there, they were very scared to accept us because they would shoot him, but his grandchildren and son, you know, persuaded him to save us, and he gave us, gave us shelter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long would you stay in one place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in that farm, we stayed a long time, maybe six months. But in between, we used to sleep in forest. It's the same thing all over again. You sleep in the forest, you find a farmer. It was an ongoing thing until the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we would eat with the farmer who would bring us food in the daytime. And the farmers, these particular two farmers I'm talking, provide us the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the daytime?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the daytime, when we were in the bunker, they would give us the food because the stables were there. We weren't looking for great food, as long as you had bread and pork or whatever they gave you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Forget clothing. We wore the same clothes. You washed them, and, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you wash them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmer would take them upstairs and wash them.

speaker: interviewer
text: He would wash them for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I mean, you sleep on hay. You sleep on the ground. I mean, it's very difficult to, to, you know, to believe and describe, you know, how you sleep and how you survive and what you eat, what you wear. The mission, you know, our goal was to stay alive and be alive, you know, not to be caught and go to any gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with any other members of your family during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family members were dead. We knew already from the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Father and mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father and mother was in one of these farms hidden by this Felix Stiponski, the first farmer, the farmer that took them out from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: They stayed the whole time with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time, not the whole time, most of the time. Then they escaped to another farm, then we got contact together, then we came back to the, to the city. We, we were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about in the wintertime? How did things go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No different than the summertime. Actually, it was better because it was cooler. It was not so hot to stay in the bunker. And, and at night we slept with the cattle, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you dig the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you dig the bunker or did the farmer?

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he dug. They dug the bunkers that were there, you know. They had the bunkers already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was, what was the configuration in size?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about 8 feet by 8 feet, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how deep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, maybe 12 feet deep.

speaker: interviewer
text: 12 feet deep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very deep. It was quite deep.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a lot of digging.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, probably a lot of digging. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, the bunker was underneath, you know-- I don't know if you're familiar with the Russian farms, you know the farms like in America, a lot of land, very primitive buildings. The outside is stables, you know, they go underneath the ground. And some of the [NON-ENGLISH], what they call bunkers, were already probably dug in advance because they would save potatoes for the winter and some of the fruits, but they used them as a Frigidaire.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So they were like a root cellar for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a root cellar, you know. They were a, a converted root cellar. A beet cellar, so things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were already dug.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were already dug.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told you about going to Poland, and turning back to the, to this place that we went to. First to Pocking, which is in Bavaria. We stayed there for a few months. And there was a lot of Zionist movements there, you know. We had a school over there. Then we moved to Feldafing outside of Munich, which we lived in displace camp. And my brother and I got a little tutoring from some ex-German teachers, professors, we were able to be getting to the school in Munich, Technische, and it's called the Munich Technische Institute. We were there till-- going to school till November '47. In November '47, we-- my mother had three sisters in Boston. My dad had aunts and uncles in New York. So we came on a Marine boat to New York and decided to go to Boston. Arrived to Boston. One of my mother's sisters took us into her house. It wasn't a big house. We managed to get-- my folks got a job. I got a job, good job. Was before Thanksgiving, taking legs off turkeys, you know, cutting off the legs of turkeys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Before you go on with your American experience, was there anything you would like to tell us about Pocking or the other place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean it was-- in Pocking was a displaced persons camp. Was a lot of activity, Zionist activity, to move people to Israel. I was basically interested in a little education. Sure, life was quite [? idle, ?] tried to read, catch up on my reading, catch up on my education. And was activities, was different Zionist organizations, you know, different groups, you know, the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH], the general Zionist party. And there was the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH], the labor parties. We belonged to the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the right party, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they want you to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they, they, they want to go every-- it isn't that they wanted to go. People wanted to go to Israel, but we felt that we wanted to get a little more educated, you know, so we went to Munich to this school. We had stood in meetings. We were very, we were very organized in a way of up to date, very concerned about, you know, Israel and the liberation of Israel and the ship, and on this ship was a lot of people leaving for Israel. As students, we did a lot of work working for the organizations, the [NON-ENGLISH], which is helping Jews escape to Israel, you know, doing some--

speaker: interviewer
text: The [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a lot of activities, but to pinpoint one-- because every activity going on every day was basically different.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were your accommodations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where, at the, at the displace camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Feldafing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Feldafing, compared to the farms, was quite good, was first class. So we had one room, you know, a common kitchen with a lot of-- 50 people. We had a room and a bed to sleep in, and actually it was a nice town we were living. It was one of the nicer towns in Germany. It was not a camp. It was old villas where, I believe, the German officer used to go on vacation on the Starnberg Lake. It's a lake. Feldafing resides-- I don't know-- about 15 minutes from Munich, you know, by train maybe 20 minutes. And you go to the lake. I believe they made a brewery for, for beer over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents there with you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents came in to the-- we joined them in Pocking. And my parents came in. And they decided that they just as soon go to-- my mother's sisters and nieces start writing letters. They want us to go to America, you know. And we decided, the folks decided to come to America. And as I told you, I got a job for a few days. And then I got another job as a stock boy in a dental company. At night I went to school. I had a part-time job. I took in a course, one semester in economics. And I worked at that company since 1948 till 1991. I was able to acquire the company from my bosses in 1956. It was a little company with about, I would say, seven, eight employees doing about $200,000 a year business. And my brother went into the Marines after he came here, you know. And then he got a job in the clothing industry, and then he came into [INAUDIBLE] business. And when I retired, the company was over 3,000 people, the largest in our field. And now I'm retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: In May. In May '91.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were living where when you had the company?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're now living--

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in my residence in Florida, since, you know, I established my [INAUDIBLE] in Florida in 1991. In the summers, I go to the Cape.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have four children, eight grandchildren. My children's name is Fredrick. He went to Brown University in law school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife was born in, in Egypt, in Cairo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in one of my customer's offices. It was a professor at Harvard, and she was an exchange student from Cairo, Egypt. So I met her, I met her before Christmas. We were married January 20, six weeks later, exactly 40 years ago. We still together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say anything overall about your experiences, a message or some feelings you have about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message is quite simple. It seems like the people could live together in peace and build a good life for themselves and their neighbors, you know, for the communities. It doesn't take more than one crazy man to destroy the world, to try to destroy the world and cause so much [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sorrow. The acts that I see the Germans do is beyond people's or human understanding. And, and It tells you how hate, envy, greed, what it can do to people. So hopefully, the survivors, most can tell their story, enough for the victims who were sacrificed for a better world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your life with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So long. This picture of my mother was taken, I believe, in 1925 in Zdolbunow or Kostopol.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken after liberation. Left to right is my father, my mother, and a friend of theirs from our town, Zdolbunow. His name is Gamberg. They all dead now. Right to left, the third is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother is fourth left to right, next to Gamberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken right after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was taken in Zdolbunow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Go ahead, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Haika. The picture was taken Kostopol in 1925. In 1942, her and her two daughters and her husband were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Germans. This is a picture of my dad and my-- my parents, my father, my mother, at my son's bar mitzvah about 24 years ago. They both escaped the Zdolbunow ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Picture was taken in Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my daughter Hedy, taken in 1995 at Cape Cod in the summer with her boyfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my son, Frederick, and his fiance, Nancy, taken in 1993, a day before their wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my daughter Cindy, and her husband, Jeff Braun. They were married in 1982, and this picture was taken in Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my daughter Elizabeth and her husband, Larry. It was taken in 1984, the day of their wedding. This is a picture of myself and my brother at a temple [NON-ENGLISH] in 1983 where we went to [INAUDIBLE] of the survivors. The Shoah week we celebrate. We're not celebrating, we are--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brookline, Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
